question: My employer has not paid my holiday pay. What can I do?

sources.title: If your employer hasn’t paid you what they owe you

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

If your employer has not paid you for holiday days you have taken, you may be entitled to receive holiday pay. Holiday pay is the money you should get for the statutory annual leave you are entitled to under UK law. You should first check your employment contract and any company policies to confirm your entitlement to holiday pay. If you believe you are owed holiday pay, you should raise the issue with your employer and ask them to pay what they owe you.

If talking to your employer does not resolve the issue, you can raise a formal grievance with your employer about the unpaid holiday pay. If the problem remains unresolved, you may be able to take legal action, such as making a claim to an employment tribunal for unlawful deduction from wages. It is important to keep any evidence that supports your claim, such as your contract, payslips, and records of the holiday you took.

Step 1: Check your contract and company policies for holiday pay entitlement.  
Step 2: Talk to your employer and explain the issue, providing any evidence you have.  
Step 3: If necessary, raise a formal grievance with your employer.  
Step 4: Consider seeking advice from ACAS, Citizens Advice, or a qualified solicitor if the issue is not resolved.  
Step 5: As a last resort, you may be able to make a claim to an employment tribunal.

Sources used:
- https://www.citizensadvice.org.uk/work/pay/problems-getting-paid/
- https://www.gov.uk/holiday-entitlement-rights/holiday-pay-the-basics

question: How is holiday pay calculated if I work irregular hours?

sources.title: How much holiday someone gets – Holiday entitlement

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

If you work irregular hours, your holiday pay is calculated based on your average pay over a relevant period, usually the last 52 weeks in which you were paid. To work this out, your employer will add up all the pay you received for hours worked during those weeks and divide it by the total number of hours you worked. This gives an average hourly rate, which is then used to calculate your holiday pay. For irregular-hours workers, all holiday leave must be paid at this ‘normal’ rate of pay, which includes regular overtime and commission but usually excludes bonuses.

Employers cannot include holiday pay within your hourly rate (known as ‘rolled-up holiday pay’) for regular-hours workers, but this may be allowed for irregular-hours or part-year workers if their leave year began before 31 March 2024. If you have questions about your holiday pay calculation, you can contact ACAS for guidance.

Step 1: Your employer calculates your average hourly pay over the last 52 weeks you were paid.  
Step 2: They multiply this average hourly rate by the number of hours of holiday you take.  
Step 3: This amount is your holiday pay for the time off.

Sources used:
- https://www.gov.uk/holiday-entitlement-rights/holiday-pay-the-basics
- https://www.acas.org.uk/checking-holiday-entitlement

question: Can my employer include holiday pay in my hourly rate?
answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

In the UK, employers generally cannot include holiday pay in your hourly rate, a practice known as ‘rolled-up holiday pay’. This means your holiday pay should be paid separately from your normal wages. However, there is an exception for workers with irregular hours or part-year workers: employers can use rolled-up holiday pay for these workers unless their leave year began on or before 31 March 2024.

If you work irregular hours or part-year, your holiday pay should be calculated based on the hours you have worked or using an average of your hours. For more detailed guidance or specific questions, you can contact Acas.

Step 1: Check if you are a worker with irregular hours or part-year employment.
Step 2: Confirm when your leave year started.
Step 3: If your employer is including holiday pay in your hourly rate and you do not fall under the exception, you may want to raise this with them or seek advice from Acas.

Sources used:
- https://www.gov.uk/holiday-entitlement-rights/holiday-pay-the-basics
- https://www.gov.uk/holiday-entitlement-rights

question: I have taken annual leave but did not get paid for it. What are my rights?

sources.title: If your employer hasn’t paid you what they owe you

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

If you have taken annual leave but were not paid for it, you may be entitled to holiday pay. Under UK law, workers have a right to paid holiday, and if your employer has not paid you for the holiday days you took, this could be a problem. You should first check your employment contract and company policies to understand your entitlement to holiday pay. If you believe you are owed holiday pay, you should gather any evidence such as your contract, payslips, and records of the leave you took.

If your employer refuses to pay you for your holiday, you can raise the issue with them directly, showing any evidence you have. If this does not resolve the matter, you may need to seek further advice or take formal action. It is important to act promptly to protect your rights.

Step 1: Check your contract and company policies for holiday pay entitlement.  
Step 2: Collect evidence of your taken leave and unpaid holiday pay.  
Step 3: Contact your employer to request payment, providing your evidence.  
Step 4: If unresolved, seek advice from ACAS, Citizens Advice, or a qualified solicitor.

Sources used:
- https://www.citizensadvice.org.uk/work/pay/problems-getting-paid/

question: How much holiday am I legally entitled to in the UK?

sources.title: How much holiday someone gets – Holiday entitlement

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

In the UK, workers are legally entitled to a minimum of 5.6 weeks' paid holiday per year. This applies whether you work full-time, part-time, or under a zero-hours contract. The 5.6 weeks include bank holidays if your contract states this. For example, if you work five days a week, this equates to 28 days of paid holiday annually. If you work part-time, your holiday entitlement is proportional to the hours you work, ensuring part-time workers are not treated less favourably than full-time workers.

If you have been employed for less than a full year, you are entitled to a proportion of the full holiday entitlement based on the length of your employment. Your holiday entitlement starts accruing from your first day of work, including periods such as probation, sick leave, or statutory leave like maternity or paternity leave. Some employers may offer more than the statutory minimum, known as enhanced or contractual holiday entitlement, which will be detailed in your contract.

Step 1: Check your employment contract or written statement of employment particulars to see your holiday entitlement and whether bank holidays are included.  
Step 2: If unsure, speak to your manager, HR department, or employer for clarification.  
Step 3: Use the GOV.UK holiday calculator or contact the Acas helpline for further guidance.

Sources used:
- https://www.acas.org.uk/checking-holiday-entitlement

question: Does holiday continue to build up while I am off sick?
answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

Yes, holiday entitlement continues to build up while you are off work sick. Under UK employment law, workers have the right to accrue their statutory holiday entitlement during periods of sickness absence. This means that even if you are not working due to illness, your holiday entitlement is still increasing as if you were working normally.

If you have any concerns about your holiday entitlement or how it is calculated during sick leave, you can discuss this with your employer or contact the Acas helpline for further guidance.

Step 1: Check your employment contract or company policy on holiday entitlement during sickness.  
Step 2: Keep records of your sickness absence and holiday entitlement.  
Step 3: If there is a dispute, raise it with your employer or seek advice from Acas or Citizens Advice.

Sources used:
- https://www.gov.uk/holiday-entitlement-rights
- https://www.acas.org.uk/holiday-sickness-leave

question: What happens to my unused holiday when I leave my job?

sources.title: Why final pay is different – Final pay when someone leaves a job

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

When you leave your job, you are entitled to be paid for any unused holiday entitlement that you have accrued but not taken. This payment is usually included in your final pay. Your employer should calculate how much holiday pay you are owed based on the amount of holiday you have built up during your employment. The final pay may also include other elements like notice pay or deductions, but the holiday pay should be clearly shown on your payslip.

If you think your employer has not paid you correctly for your unused holiday, you should first speak to them to clarify how your final pay was calculated. If you do not receive a payslip or explanation, you can contact the Acas helpline for guidance.

Step 1: Check your final payslip to see if unused holiday pay is included.  
Step 2: Ask your employer for clarification if you are unsure or if it is missing.  
Step 3: Contact Acas or Citizens Advice if you cannot resolve the issue with your employer.

Sources used:
- https://www.gov.uk/holiday-entitlement-rights
- https://www.acas.org.uk/final-pay-when-someone-leaves-a-job

question: Can my employer refuse my holiday request?
answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

Your employer can refuse your holiday request, but they must have a good reason for doing so. While you have a legal right to paid annual leave, employers can manage when holidays are taken to ensure the business runs smoothly. For example, they might refuse holiday requests during busy periods or if too many employees want the same time off. However, your employer should give you notice of refusal in a timely manner, ideally before you make any travel or other plans.

If you disagree with your employer’s decision, you can try to resolve the dispute by discussing it with them or following your workplace’s grievance procedure. If the issue remains unresolved, you may seek advice from ACAS or Citizens Advice.

Step 1: Check your employment contract or company holiday policy for any specific rules about holiday requests.  
Step 2: Submit your holiday request in writing, giving reasonable notice.  
Step 3: If your request is refused, ask your employer for the reasons and try to negotiate alternative dates.  
Step 4: If you believe your rights are not being respected, contact ACAS or Citizens Advice for guidance.

Sources used:
- https://www.gov.uk/holiday-entitlement-rights
- https://www.gov.uk/holiday-entitlement-rights/holiday-pay-the-basics

question: Can my employer force me to take holiday?
answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

Your employer can require you to take holiday at certain times, such as during a shutdown period or if you have accrued a lot of untaken holiday. However, they must give you notice at least twice as long as the length of the holiday they want you to take. For example, if they want you to take one week off, they must give you at least two weeks' notice. You cannot be forced to take holiday without this proper notice.

If you think your employer is not following the rules about holiday entitlement or pay, you can raise the issue with them or seek advice from organisations like ACAS or Citizens Advice.

Step 1: Check your contract and your employer’s holiday policy for any specific rules about taking holiday.  
Step 2: If your employer wants you to take holiday, ensure they give you the correct notice period.  
Step 3: If you have concerns, contact ACAS or Citizens Advice for guidance.

Sources used:
- https://www.gov.uk/holiday-entitlement-rights
- https://www.gov.uk/holiday-entitlement-rights/holiday-pay-the-basics

question: Do part-time workers get holiday pay?

sources.title: How much holiday someone gets – Holiday entitlement

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

Yes, part-time workers in the UK are entitled to holiday pay. By law, all workers, including part-time employees, have the right to 5.6 weeks of statutory paid holiday per year. The amount of holiday pay a part-time worker receives is proportional to the hours they work, ensuring they are not treated less favourably than full-time workers. If an employer offers more than the statutory minimum holiday entitlement to full-time workers, they must also provide part-time workers with a proportionate increase.

Holiday entitlement starts accruing from the first day of work and includes periods such as probation, sick leave, and statutory leave like maternity or paternity leave. If a worker is employed for less than a full year, their holiday entitlement will be calculated on a pro-rata basis.

Step 1: Check your employment contract to see your specific holiday entitlement and whether it includes any enhanced holiday beyond the statutory minimum.  
Step 2: Calculate your holiday entitlement based on the hours you work, using the 5.6 weeks statutory entitlement as a baseline.  
Step 3: If you have irregular hours or work part of the year, look into specific rules that apply to your situation or contact ACAS for guidance.

Sources used:
- https://www.acas.org.uk/checking-holiday-entitlement
- https://www.gov.uk/holiday-entitlement-rights/holiday-pay-the-basics

question: Can my employer pay me instead of giving me holiday?

sources.title: Why final pay is different – Final pay when someone leaves a job

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

In the UK, workers have a legal right to paid annual leave, and employers must provide this leave rather than simply paying you instead of giving you time off. However, there are some exceptions. For example, if you leave your job and have unused holiday entitlement, your employer should pay you for that untaken holiday as part of your final pay. This payment is sometimes called "holiday pay in lieu."

Employers cannot include holiday pay within your hourly rate (known as "rolled-up holiday pay") for most workers, especially if your leave year started after 31 March 2024. If you have questions about how holiday pay is calculated or your rights regarding holiday entitlement, you can contact Acas for guidance.

Step 1: Check your contract and holiday entitlement to understand your rights.  
Step 2: If you think your employer is not providing your legal holiday entitlement, raise the issue with them or use their grievance procedure.  
Step 3: Contact Acas or Citizens Advice for further help if needed.

Sources used:
- https://www.gov.uk/holiday-entitlement-rights
- https://www.gov.uk/holiday-entitlement-rights/holiday-pay-the-basics
- https://www.acas.org.uk/final-pay-when-someone-leaves-a-job

question: Is rolled-up holiday pay legal in the UK?
answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

Rolled-up holiday pay, which means including an amount for holiday pay within the hourly rate, is generally not allowed for most workers in the UK. However, employers can use rolled-up holiday pay for workers with irregular hours or part-year workers, but only if their leave year began before 1 April 2024. For leave years starting on or after 1 April 2024, rolled-up holiday pay is not permitted.

If you have questions about how holiday pay should be calculated, especially for workers without fixed hours or pay, you can contact the Advisory, Conciliation and Arbitration Service (Acas) for guidance.

Step 1: Check when your leave year started to see if rolled-up holiday pay applies.  
Step 2: If your employer uses rolled-up holiday pay and your leave year started after 31 March 2024, this is likely not legal.  
Step 3: Contact Acas or seek further advice if you believe your holiday pay is not being calculated correctly.

Sources used:
- https://www.gov.uk/holiday-entitlement-rights/holiday-pay-the-basics
- https://www.gov.uk/holiday-entitlement-rights

question: My employer has not paid my wages on payday. What should I do?

sources.title: If your employer hasn’t paid you what they owe you

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

If your employer has not paid your wages on the agreed payday, you should first talk to them to find out why. It might be a mistake they can fix quickly. If you have a payslip, check it to understand the issue better. If talking to your employer does not resolve the problem, you can raise a formal grievance if your employer has a grievance procedure. Keep any evidence you have, such as contracts or payslips, to support your case.

If your employer still does not pay you what you are owed, you may be able to take legal action. You can make a claim to an employment tribunal for unlawful deduction of wages, but you must do this within 3 months minus one day from the date the wages were due. If you miss this deadline, you might be able to claim through a small claims court instead. It is usually best to try to resolve the issue without legal action if you plan to continue working for your employer.

Step 1: Talk to your employer or payroll department to ask why you haven’t been paid.  
Step 2: Check your payslip and contract to understand what you are owed.  
Step 3: Raise a formal grievance if talking doesn’t work and your employer has a grievance procedure.  
Step 4: Consider legal action through an employment tribunal or small claims court if the problem remains unresolved.

Sources used:
- https://www.citizensadvice.org.uk/work/pay/problems-getting-paid/

question: My employer deducted money from my wages without explaining why.

sources.title: If your employer hasn’t paid you what they owe you

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

Your employer can only deduct money from your wages if it is allowed by your contract or if you have agreed in writing. For example, deductions can be made for things like damaged property or missing money from the till, but only if there is a written agreement and certain rules are followed. These rules include not deducting more than 10% of your pay before tax on each pay day, starting deductions within 12 months of noticing the issue, and providing you with written details of the deduction each time money is taken.

If your employer deducted money without explaining why or without your agreement, you should first check your contract and any written agreements to see if such deductions are allowed. If you believe the deduction was not lawful, you can seek advice from an adviser or a trade union representative if you have one.

Step 1: Review your employment contract and any written agreements about deductions.  
Step 2: Ask your employer to explain the deduction and provide written details.  
Step 3: If you disagree or don’t get a satisfactory explanation, seek advice from an employment adviser or trade union.  
Step 4: If necessary, consider raising a grievance with your employer or taking further action.

Sources used:
- https://www.citizensadvice.org.uk/work/pay/problems-getting-paid/

question: I think I am being paid below minimum wage. What can I do?

sources.title: If your employer hasn’t paid you what they owe you
What is the minimum wage – National Minimum Wage

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

If you think you are being paid below the National Minimum Wage (NMW) or National Living Wage (NLW), you should first check the current rates for your age group or apprenticeship status. Employers must pay at least the minimum wage by law. If your pay is below this, your employer may be breaking the law. You can check the rates on GOV.UK or ACAS websites to confirm what you should be paid.

You should talk to your employer or payroll department to raise the issue and show any evidence you have, such as payslips or your contract. If this does not resolve the problem, you can raise a formal grievance with your employer. If your employer still does not pay you correctly, you may be able to take legal action, such as making a claim to an employment tribunal for unlawful deduction of wages. Keep in mind there is a time limit of 3 months minus one day from when you should have been paid to start a tribunal claim.

Step 1: Check the current minimum wage rates for your age or apprenticeship status.  
Step 2: Talk to your employer or payroll department about your pay concerns.  
Step 3: Raise a formal grievance if the issue is not resolved informally.  
Step 4: Consider making a claim to an employment tribunal if your employer refuses to pay the correct wages.

Sources used:
- https://www.citizensadvice.org.uk/work/pay/problems-getting-paid/
- https://www.acas.org.uk/national-minimum-wage-entitlement

question: What can I do if my employer pays me late every month?

sources.title: If your employer hasn’t paid you what they owe you

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

If your employer pays you late every month, you should first talk to them to find out why the payments are delayed. It’s important to raise the issue early and explain how it affects you. If your employer has made a mistake, they should correct it and pay you as soon as possible. You can also check your payslips to understand exactly what is happening with your pay.

If talking to your employer doesn’t resolve the problem, you can raise a formal grievance with them if they have a grievance procedure. If the issue continues and you are not paid what you are owed, you may be able to take legal action, such as making a claim to an employment tribunal for unauthorised deduction from wages. However, this should be a last resort, especially if you want to continue working for your employer.

Step 1: Talk to your employer or payroll department about the late payments.  
Step 2: Check your payslips and employment contract to understand your pay terms.  
Step 3: Raise a formal grievance if informal talks don’t work.  
Step 4: Consider legal action, such as an employment tribunal claim, if the problem persists.

Sources used:
- https://www.citizensadvice.org.uk/work/pay/problems-getting-paid/

question: Can my employer make deductions from my wages?

sources.title: If your employer hasn’t paid you what they owe you

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

Your employer can only make deductions from your wages if it is allowed by your contract or you have agreed in writing. For example, deductions can be made for overpayments, damaged property, missing money from the till, or the cost of a uniform, but only if your contract says so or you have given written permission. There are extra rules if deductions are for missing money from the till or damaged stock: your employer must have a written agreement, cannot deduct more than 10% of your pay before tax on each payday, must start deductions within 12 months of noticing the issue, and must provide written details of each deduction.

If you think your employer has made deductions they shouldn’t have, you should check your contract and any written agreements. If you disagree with a deduction, you should talk to your employer or seek advice from an adviser.

Step 1: Check your employment contract and any written agreements about deductions.  
Step 2: If you disagree with a deduction, talk to your employer and explain why.  
Step 3: If the issue is not resolved, seek advice from a trade union representative or an employment adviser.

Sources used:
- https://www.citizensadvice.org.uk/work/pay/problems-getting-paid/

question: My employer has not paid me for overtime. Is that legal?

sources.title: If your employer hasn’t paid you what they owe you
What is the minimum wage – National Minimum Wage

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

In the UK, whether your employer must pay you for overtime depends on your contract and the terms agreed upon. There is no legal requirement for employers to pay extra for overtime unless your contract or workplace policy says so. However, your total pay (including overtime) must at least meet the National Minimum Wage or National Living Wage for all hours worked. If your employer is not paying you for overtime hours worked, you should check your contract and payslips to understand what you are entitled to.

If you believe you are owed pay for overtime and your employer has not paid you, you should first raise the issue with them directly. If this does not resolve the problem, you may be able to make a claim for unpaid wages through an employment tribunal or small claims court, but there are time limits for doing so (usually within 3 months minus one day from when the pay was due).

Step 1: Check your employment contract and payslips to confirm your entitlement to overtime pay.  
Step 2: Talk to your employer or payroll department to clarify why overtime pay has not been made.  
Step 3: If unresolved, seek advice from ACAS, Citizens Advice, or a qualified adviser about making a claim for unpaid wages.

Sources used:
- https://www.citizensadvice.org.uk/work/pay/problems-getting-paid/
- https://www.acas.org.uk/national-minimum-wage-entitlement

question: What should be included on my payslip?

sources.title: If your employer hasn’t paid you what they owe you
What is the minimum wage – National Minimum Wage

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

A payslip should clearly show details about your pay and any deductions made. This typically includes your gross pay (the total amount before deductions), the amount of tax and National Insurance contributions deducted, any other deductions such as pension contributions or student loan repayments, and your net pay (the amount you take home). It should also state the pay period it covers and the date of payment.

Having a detailed payslip helps you check that you are being paid correctly, including whether the right amount of tax and National Minimum Wage or National Living Wage is being applied. If you notice any discrepancies or deductions you don’t agree with, you should raise this with your employer or seek advice.

Step 1: Check your payslip each time you are paid to ensure all details are correct.  
Step 2: If you spot any errors or deductions you don’t understand, ask your employer for clarification.  
Step 3: If the issue is not resolved, consider seeking advice from an employment adviser or Citizens Advice.

Sources used:
- https://www.citizensadvice.org.uk/work/pay/problems-getting-paid/
- https://www.acas.org.uk/national-minimum-wage-entitlement

question: Can my employer withhold my final pay when I leave?

sources.title: Why final pay is different – Final pay when someone leaves a job
If your employer hasn’t paid you what they owe you

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

Your employer should pay your final pay on your usual payday, even if you leave partway through the pay period. Final pay can include your normal wages, any holiday pay owed, redundancy pay, and deductions for things like training courses if applicable. Employers must clearly explain how your final pay was calculated, usually on your payslip. If you do not receive your final payslip or final pay on time, you should contact your employer to ask for clarification.

If your employer withholds your final pay without a valid reason, you can raise the issue with them first. If this does not resolve the problem, you may be able to take further action, such as contacting ACAS or Citizens Advice for help or considering an employment tribunal claim, but this should be a last resort.

Step 1: Check your payslip and final pay details to understand what you should be paid.  
Step 2: Contact your employer to request your final pay and payslip if you have not received them.  
Step 3: If your employer refuses or delays payment without explanation, seek advice from ACAS or Citizens Advice.  
Step 4: Consider formal action if necessary, such as an employment tribunal claim, within the required time limits.

Sources used:
- https://www.acas.org.uk/final-pay-when-someone-leaves-a-job
- https://www.citizensadvice.org.uk/work/pay/problems-getting-paid/

question: What can I do if my employer owes me commission?

sources.title: If your employer hasn’t paid you what they owe you
What is the minimum wage – National Minimum Wage

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

If your employer owes you commission, this is considered part of your wages and you have the right to be paid what you are owed. First, check your employment contract or any agreement that outlines how commission is calculated and paid. Gather any evidence you have, such as sales records or emails confirming the commission terms. You should try to resolve the issue by talking to your employer and showing them your evidence.

If talking to your employer does not work, you can raise a formal grievance with your employer if they have a grievance procedure. If the problem remains unresolved, you may be able to take legal action by making a claim to an employment tribunal for unpaid wages or an unlawful deduction from wages. You must start this process within 3 months minus one day from when the commission was due. If you miss this deadline, you might be able to claim unpaid commission through a small claims court instead.

Step 1: Check your contract and gather evidence of the commission owed.  
Step 2: Talk to your employer to try and resolve the issue informally.  
Step 3: Raise a formal grievance if informal talks fail.  
Step 4: Consider making a claim to an employment tribunal or small claims court if necessary, keeping in mind the time limits.

Sources used:
- https://www.citizensadvice.org.uk/work/pay/problems-getting-paid/
- https://www.acas.org.uk/national-minimum-wage-entitlement

question: Is it legal for my employer to charge me for training?

sources.title: If your employer hasn’t paid you what they owe you
What is the minimum wage – National Minimum Wage

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

In the UK, your employer can require you to do training, including mandatory training, as part of your job. However, even if your contract says you won’t be paid for this training, your employer must still pay you at least the National Minimum Wage or National Living Wage for the time you spend training. This means they cannot charge you for training by deducting pay below the legal minimum wage.

If you believe your employer is charging you for training or not paying you properly for training time, you should check your contract and gather any evidence of what you have been asked to pay or not paid for. You can then raise the issue with your employer or seek advice from organisations like Citizens Advice or ACAS.

Step 1: Review your employment contract and any company policies about training and pay.  
Step 2: Check if your pay for training meets or exceeds the National Minimum Wage or National Living Wage.  
Step 3: If you think your employer is not complying with the law, contact Citizens Advice or ACAS for guidance on how to proceed.

Sources used:
- https://www.citizensadvice.org.uk/work/pay/problems-getting-paid/
- https://www.acas.org.uk/national-minimum-wage-entitlement

question: My employer says they overpaid me and wants the money back. What are my rights?

sources.title: If your employer hasn’t paid you what they owe you

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

If your employer says they overpaid you and wants the money back, they can deduct the overpaid amount from your wages, but only if you have agreed to this in writing or if it is stated in your contract. If you do not agree that you were overpaid, you should explain why and ask your employer to pay you what you believe you are owed. If you were overpaid but cannot afford to repay the full amount immediately, you can ask your employer to take smaller deductions over a longer period.

There are also rules your employer must follow if they deduct money for overpayments. For example, they cannot deduct more than 10% of your pay before tax on each payday, must start deductions within 12 months of noticing the overpayment, and must give you written details of each deduction. If you think your employer has made unlawful deductions, you should check your contract and any written agreements and consider seeking advice.

Step 1: Check your employment contract and any written agreements about deductions.  
Step 2: If you disagree with the overpayment claim, explain your reasons to your employer.  
Step 3: If you agree but cannot repay immediately, ask to spread the repayments.  
Step 4: If deductions are made, ensure they comply with the legal limits and notice requirements.  
Step 5: If you believe deductions are unlawful, seek advice from an adviser or Citizens Advice.

Sources used:
- https://www.citizensadvice.org.uk/work/pay/problems-getting-paid/

question: Can my employer change my pay without telling me?

sources.title: If your employer hasn’t paid you what they owe you
What is the minimum wage – National Minimum Wage

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

Your employer should not change your pay without informing you. Any changes to your pay should be agreed upon, ideally in writing, as part of your contract or through a formal variation to your contract. If your employer deducts money from your wages, they must have your agreement in writing or it must be stated in your contract. There are specific rules about deductions, such as limits on how much can be deducted and requirements to provide written details each pay day.

If your employer changes your pay without your agreement, you should first check your contract and any written agreements you have. If you believe your pay has been unlawfully deducted or changed, you can raise the issue with your employer or seek advice from an employment adviser. If the problem is not resolved, you may be able to take legal action, but it is usually best to try to resolve the issue informally first.

Step 1: Check your employment contract and any written agreements about pay and deductions.  
Step 2: Talk to your employer or payroll department to clarify any changes or deductions.  
Step 3: If you disagree with the change and cannot resolve it, seek advice from ACAS, Citizens Advice, or a qualified adviser.

Sources used:
- https://www.citizensadvice.org.uk/work/pay/problems-getting-paid/
- https://www.acas.org.uk/national-minimum-wage-entitlement

question: What should I do if I have not been paid for work I have already done?

sources.title: If your employer hasn’t paid you what they owe you

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

If your employer hasn’t paid you for work you have already done, you should first talk to them to find out why you haven’t been paid what you expected. Check your payslip if you have one to understand the issue better. If it was a mistake, ask your employer to pay you straight away and not wait until your next pay day. If talking to your employer doesn’t solve the problem, you can raise a formal grievance if your employer has a grievance procedure.

If the problem continues, you may be able to take legal action. You can make a claim to an employment tribunal for unpaid wages or unlawful deductions, but you must do this within 3 months minus a day from when you should have been paid. If you miss this deadline, you might be able to claim through a small claims court instead. It is usually best to try to resolve the issue while continuing to work, as refusing to work might breach your contract.

Step 1: Talk to your employer or payroll department to clarify the issue.  
Step 2: Check your payslip and employment contract for details.  
Step 3: Raise a formal grievance if necessary.  
Step 4: Consider legal action through an employment tribunal or small claims court if the issue is not resolved.

Sources used:
- https://www.citizensadvice.org.uk/work/pay/problems-getting-paid/

question: I was dismissed without a meeting. Is that unfair dismissal?
answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

Being dismissed without a meeting can be a concern because employers are generally expected to follow a fair and reasonable procedure before dismissing someone. This usually includes investigating the situation and giving the employee a chance to respond, often through a meeting or hearing. If your employer dismissed you without any meeting or opportunity to explain your side, this could mean they did not follow a fair procedure.

However, whether the dismissal is unfair depends on other factors too, such as whether the employer had a valid reason for dismissal and acted reasonably in the circumstances. If you have been employed for at least two years, you may have the right to ask for a written explanation of why you were dismissed. You can also appeal the dismissal if your employer has an appeal process.

Step 1: Check if you have been employed for at least two years to understand your rights regarding unfair dismissal claims.  
Step 2: Request a written statement from your employer explaining the reasons for your dismissal if you have not already received one.  
Step 3: Consider whether your employer followed a fair procedure, including whether you were given a chance to respond before dismissal.  
Step 4: If you believe the dismissal was unfair, you may want to raise a grievance with your employer or seek advice from ACAS or Citizens Advice.

Sources used:
- https://www.acas.org.uk/dismissals
- https://www.gov.uk/dismissal

question: My employer dismissed me and refused to let me appeal.
answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

If your employer has dismissed you and refused to let you appeal, this could be a concern because a fair dismissal process usually includes the right to appeal the decision. Employers are expected to follow a fair and reasonable procedure when dismissing someone, which typically involves giving you the chance to challenge or appeal the dismissal. Without this, the dismissal process may not be considered fair.

You should ask your employer for the reasons for your dismissal in writing if you haven't received them already, especially if you have been employed for at least two years or are on maternity leave. If you believe the dismissal was unfair because you were not allowed to appeal, you might be able to make a claim for unfair dismissal to an employment tribunal. However, it is important to note that the employer must have a valid reason for dismissal and act reasonably in the circumstances.

Step 1: Request a written statement from your employer explaining the reasons for your dismissal.  
Step 2: Check if you have the right to appeal under your contract or company policy.  
Step 3: Consider seeking advice from ACAS or Citizens Advice about your situation and whether you can make an unfair dismissal claim.

Sources used:
- https://www.acas.org.uk/dismissals
- https://www.gov.uk/dismissal

question: Can I be dismissed without notice?
answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

In the UK, your employer can dismiss you without notice only in certain situations, such as gross misconduct (for example, violence or theft). This is called summary dismissal. In most other cases, your employer must give you at least the notice period stated in your contract or the statutory minimum notice period, whichever is longer. The employer must also have a valid reason for dismissal, act reasonably, and follow a fair procedure.

If you are dismissed, you have the right to ask for a written statement explaining the reasons for your dismissal if you have worked for your employer for at least 2 years (or 1 year if you started before 6 April 2012). Employers must provide this within 14 days of your request. Certain employees, such as those on maternity leave, have additional rights to receive this statement regardless of length of service.

Step 1: Check your employment contract for your notice period.  
Step 2: Consider whether your dismissal was for a valid reason and if the employer followed a fair process.  
Step 3: If you want, ask your employer in writing for a written statement of reasons for dismissal.  
Step 4: If you believe your dismissal was unfair, you may want to seek advice from ACAS or Citizens Advice.

Sources used:
- https://www.gov.uk/dismissal
- https://www.acas.org.uk/dismissals

question: What is constructive dismissal?
answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

Constructive dismissal happens when an employee resigns because their employer has behaved in a way that seriously breaches their contract, making it impossible for the employee to continue working there. This could include things like a significant change to their job duties, not paying them, or creating a hostile work environment. The employee must leave the job because of this breach, not for other reasons.

For a claim of constructive dismissal to be considered, the employee usually needs to have been employed for at least two years. It is important that the employee follows a fair procedure, such as raising concerns with the employer before resigning, to show that the resignation was a last resort.

Step 1: Identify the employer’s breach of contract or serious wrongdoing.  
Step 2: Raise the issue with the employer, if possible, to seek resolution.  
Step 3: If the issue is not resolved and the breach continues, resign and state that you are leaving because of the employer’s conduct.  
Step 4: Consider seeking advice from ACAS, Citizens Advice, or a qualified solicitor before making a claim.

Sources used:
- https://www.acas.org.uk/dismissals

question: How long do I need to work somewhere to claim unfair dismissal?
answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

To claim unfair dismissal in the UK, you generally need to have worked for your employer for at least 2 years. This means you must have the legal status of an employee and have been continuously employed for that period. If you have been employed for less than 2 years, you usually cannot claim unfair dismissal unless the dismissal relates to certain automatically unfair reasons, such as discrimination or whistleblowing.

If you believe you have been unfairly dismissed after meeting the 2-year requirement, you should follow a fair procedure with your employer, including the right to appeal the dismissal. If the procedure is not fair, you may be able to make a claim to an employment tribunal.

Step 1: Check your length of continuous employment to see if you meet the 2-year threshold.  
Step 2: Review the reason and procedure for your dismissal to assess fairness.  
Step 3: Consider appealing the dismissal with your employer.  
Step 4: If unresolved, you may make a claim to an employment tribunal within the time limits.

Sources used:
- https://www.acas.org.uk/dismissals

question: My employer says I was dismissed for poor performance. What are my rights?
answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

If your employer says you were dismissed for poor performance, they must have a valid reason and act reasonably in the circumstances. This means they should have investigated the situation fully and followed a fair procedure before dismissing you. Your employer should be consistent and not dismiss you for something they have allowed other employees to do. You have the right to be told why you were dismissed, when your employment ends, your notice period, and your right to appeal the decision. It is best if this information is provided in writing.

If you have been employed for at least 2 years (or 1 year if you started before 6 April 2012), you can ask your employer for a written statement explaining the reasons for your dismissal, which they must provide within 14 days. If you believe your dismissal was unfair, you can appeal the decision with your employer. If the appeal is unsuccessful, you may be able to make a claim for unfair dismissal to an employment tribunal.

Step 1: Ask your employer for a written statement explaining the reasons for your dismissal if you have the required length of service.  
Step 2: Consider whether your employer followed a fair procedure and acted reasonably.  
Step 3: If you think the dismissal was unfair, appeal the decision with your employer.  
Step 4: If necessary, seek advice from ACAS or Citizens Advice about making a claim for unfair dismissal.

Sources used:
- https://www.gov.uk/dismissal
- https://www.acas.org.uk/dismissals

question: Can I be dismissed while on sick leave?
answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

Yes, you can be dismissed while on sick leave, but your employer must have a fair and valid reason for doing so and must act reasonably in the circumstances. They must also follow a proper process, including investigating the situation fully before dismissing you. For example, if your dismissal is related to your sickness absence, your employer should consider whether dismissal is a reasonable response and whether other options, such as adjustments or alternative roles, are possible.

If you are dismissed while on Statutory Maternity Leave, your employer must give you a written statement explaining the reasons for your dismissal, regardless of how long you have worked for them. For other types of sick leave, you have the right to ask for a written statement of reasons if you have at least two years’ continuous service (or one year if you started before 6 April 2012).

Step 1: Check if your employer has a valid reason and has acted reasonably in your dismissal while on sick leave.  
Step 2: Request a written statement of the reasons for your dismissal if you have the required length of service.  
Step 3: If you believe your dismissal was unfair, consider seeking advice from ACAS or Citizens Advice.

Sources used:
- https://www.gov.uk/dismissal
- https://www.acas.org.uk/holiday-sickness-leave

question: What should happen in a fair disciplinary process?
answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

A fair disciplinary process should be reasonable and follow a clear procedure. The employer must have a valid reason for any disciplinary action and make decisions that are balanced and consistent. Usually, the employer should follow the Acas Code of Practice on disciplinary and grievance procedures, which includes informing the employee about the issue, giving them a chance to respond, and allowing them to appeal any decision made.

The process should be transparent, and the employee should be told why disciplinary action is being considered, what evidence is involved, and what the possible outcomes are. Confidentiality should be maintained throughout, and the employee should be given the opportunity to be accompanied at disciplinary meetings if they wish.

Step 1: Employer identifies the issue and informs the employee clearly.
Step 2: Employer holds a disciplinary meeting, allowing the employee to respond.
Step 3: Employer makes a fair decision based on evidence and informs the employee.
Step 4: Employee is given the right to appeal the decision.
Step 5: Employer respects confidentiality throughout the process.

Sources used:
- https://www.acas.org.uk/dismissals

question: Can I be made redundant without consultation?
answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

In the UK, employers should consult with employees before making them redundant. Consultation is an important part of the redundancy process and helps to explore alternatives to redundancy and discuss how the redundancy will be carried out. If an employer fails to consult properly, this could make the redundancy unfair. The length and form of consultation can depend on the number of employees being made redundant.

If you are facing redundancy, your employer should inform you about the reasons and consider any alternatives. You may also have the right to a redundancy payment if you have worked for your employer for at least two years.

Step 1: Ask your employer for details about the redundancy process and whether consultation will take place.  
Step 2: If you feel consultation has not been properly conducted, seek advice from ACAS or Citizens Advice.  
Step 3: Consider your rights regarding redundancy pay and unfair dismissal claims.

Sources used:
- https://www.acas.org.uk/dismissals
- https://www.acas.org.uk/holiday-sickness-leave

question: What is the difference between redundancy and dismissal?
answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

Dismissal is when an employer ends an employee's contract, which can happen for various reasons such as poor performance, misconduct, or redundancy. Redundancy is a specific type of dismissal that occurs when an employer needs to reduce their workforce because a job or role is no longer needed, for example due to business closure or changes in work requirements.

While all redundancies are dismissals, not all dismissals are redundancies. Redundancy is usually related to the employer's operational needs, whereas other dismissals may be due to employee-related issues. Employers must follow a fair procedure and have a valid reason for any dismissal, including redundancy.

Step 1: Understand the reason for the dismissal—whether it is redundancy or another reason.  
Step 2: Check that the employer has followed a fair and reasonable procedure.  
Step 3: If you believe the dismissal was unfair, you may have the right to appeal or make a claim for unfair dismissal.

Sources used:
- https://www.acas.org.uk/dismissals

question: My employer dismissed me for raising a complaint. Is this unfair?

sources.title: If your employer hasn’t paid you what they owe you

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

If your employer dismissed you because you raised a complaint, this could potentially be an unfair dismissal, especially if the complaint was made in good faith and related to a workplace issue such as discrimination, health and safety, or other protected rights. Employers must have a valid reason for dismissal and follow a fair and reasonable procedure. Dismissing someone for raising a complaint may be seen as victimisation, which is unlawful under UK employment law.

You have the right to appeal against the dismissal and your employer should follow a fair procedure. If you believe the dismissal was unfair, you may be able to make a claim to an employment tribunal. It is important to act quickly because there are strict time limits for making such claims.

Step 1: Ask your employer for a written explanation of the reasons for your dismissal.  
Step 2: Use the appeal process if you believe the dismissal was unfair.  
Step 3: Seek advice from ACAS or Citizens Advice about your situation and consider whether to make a claim to an employment tribunal.

Sources used:
- https://www.acas.org.uk/dismissals
- https://www.citizensadvice.org.uk/work/pay/problems-getting-paid/

question: Can I challenge my dismissal at a tribunal?
answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

You can challenge your dismissal at an employment tribunal if you believe it was unfair. For a dismissal to be fair, your employer must have a valid reason, act reasonably, and follow a fair procedure. This includes investigating the situation fully and being consistent in how they treat employees. If you have been employed for at least 2 years (or 1 year if you started before 6 April 2012), you have the right to request a written statement from your employer explaining the reasons for your dismissal.

If you think your dismissal was unfair, you should first appeal the decision with your employer. If the appeal is unsuccessful, you may be able to bring a claim to an employment tribunal. Keep in mind that if you accept a settlement agreement, you usually give up the right to make an unfair dismissal claim. It is important to act quickly because there are strict time limits for making tribunal claims.

Step 1: Ask your employer for a written statement of the reasons for your dismissal if you have not already received one.  
Step 2: Consider appealing the dismissal through your employer’s internal procedures.  
Step 3: If the appeal fails, seek advice and consider making a claim to an employment tribunal within the time limit (usually 3 months minus one day from the dismissal date).

Sources used:
- https://www.acas.org.uk/dismissals
- https://www.gov.uk/dismissal

question: What should I do if I believe my dismissal was unfair?
answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

If you believe your dismissal was unfair, your employer should have followed a fair and reasonable procedure, including giving you a valid reason for dismissal and allowing you to appeal the decision. You can appeal against the dismissal with your employer, and they must handle this fairly. If you successfully appeal and get your job back, this is called a "vanishing dismissal," meaning you cannot then make a claim for unfair dismissal at an employment tribunal.

If you want to challenge the dismissal further, you may be able to make a claim for unfair dismissal to an employment tribunal. It is important to act promptly and follow the correct procedures. You can also seek advice from organisations such as ACAS or Citizens Advice to understand your options and next steps.

Step 1: Ask your employer for a written explanation of the reasons for your dismissal if you have not already received one.  
Step 2: Use your employer’s appeal process to challenge the dismissal.  
Step 3: If you are not satisfied with the outcome, consider contacting ACAS or Citizens Advice for guidance on making a claim to an employment tribunal.

Sources used:
- https://www.acas.org.uk/dismissals

question: I was treated unfairly at work because of my religion. What are my rights?
answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

Under UK law, specifically the Equality Act 2010, it is illegal for your employer or colleagues to discriminate against you because of your religion or belief. This protection covers all aspects of work, including hiring, pay, promotion, and dismissal. You are also protected if you are treated unfairly because you are associated with someone of a particular religion or because you have made a complaint about discrimination.

If you believe you have been treated unfairly due to your religion, you can raise the issue with your employer, either informally or through a formal grievance procedure. Employers are required to take complaints seriously and take steps to prevent discrimination and harassment at work. If the issue is not resolved internally, you may have the option to bring a claim to an employment tribunal.

Step 1: Document the incidents where you believe you were treated unfairly because of your religion. Step 2: Raise the issue with your employer, following their grievance procedure if available. Step 3: If unresolved, seek advice from ACAS or Citizens Advice about your options for an employment tribunal claim.

Sources used:
- https://www.gov.uk/discrimination-your-rights
- https://www.acas.org.uk/discrimination-bullying-and-harassment

question: My manager is harassing me. Is this discrimination or harassment?
answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

Harassment at work can be a form of discrimination if it is related to a protected characteristic under the Equality Act 2010, such as age, sex, race, disability, or religion. Harassment means unwanted behaviour linked to one of these protected characteristics that violates your dignity or creates an intimidating, hostile, degrading, humiliating, or offensive environment. If your manager's behaviour fits this description and is connected to a protected characteristic, it may be considered harassment and discrimination.

If the harassment is not linked to a protected characteristic but involves bullying or other inappropriate behaviour, it may still be unacceptable and your employer should take steps to address it. You can raise the issue informally or formally with your employer, who has a responsibility to prevent and handle harassment and bullying at work.

Step 1: Document the incidents of harassment, noting dates, times, and details.  
Step 2: Report the behaviour to your employer, either informally or through a formal complaint procedure.  
Step 3: If the issue is not resolved, you may seek advice from ACAS or Citizens Advice about next steps.

Sources used:
- https://www.acas.org.uk/discrimination-bullying-and-harassment
- https://www.gov.uk/discrimination-your-rights

question: What is workplace discrimination under the Equality Act 2010?
answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

Workplace discrimination under the Equality Act 2010 means treating someone unfairly or less favourably because they have a protected characteristic. These protected characteristics include age, gender reassignment, being married or in a civil partnership, pregnancy or maternity, disability, race (including colour, nationality, ethnic or national origin), religion or belief, sex, and sexual orientation. Discrimination can happen directly, such as refusing to hire someone because of their race, or indirectly, like applying a rule that disadvantages a particular group.

The law protects employees from discrimination not only because of their own protected characteristics but also if they are associated with someone who has one, or if they have complained about discrimination or supported someone else’s claim. Employers have a legal duty to prevent discrimination, take complaints seriously, and handle them properly.

Step 1: If you believe you have experienced discrimination, you can raise the issue informally with your employer or follow a formal complaint procedure.  
Step 2: Keep records of incidents and communications related to the discrimination.  
Step 3: If the issue is not resolved, you may consider seeking advice from ACAS or Citizens Advice about your options.

Sources used:
- https://www.gov.uk/discrimination-your-rights
- https://www.acas.org.uk/discrimination-bullying-and-harassment

question: Can I be treated differently because of my disability?
answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

Under UK law, specifically the Equality Act 2010, it is illegal to treat someone unfairly or differently because of their disability. Disability is a protected characteristic, meaning you have the right to be treated equally at work and in other areas such as education and public services. Employers must make reasonable adjustments to accommodate your disability and prevent discrimination.

If you believe you have been treated unfairly due to your disability, you can raise the issue with your employer, who should take your complaint seriously and take steps to prevent discrimination. You also have the option to seek advice from organisations like ACAS or Citizens Advice.

Step 1: Identify if the treatment you have experienced relates to your disability.  
Step 2: Raise the issue informally with your employer or through a formal complaint process.  
Step 3: Seek advice from ACAS or Citizens Advice if the issue is not resolved.

Sources used:
- https://www.gov.uk/discrimination-your-rights
- https://www.acas.org.uk/discrimination-bullying-and-harassment

question: What is victimisation at work?
answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

Victimisation at work happens when an employer or colleague treats you badly because you have made a complaint about discrimination, supported someone else's complaint, or raised a concern about discrimination. It is a form of unlawful discrimination under the Equality Act 2010. Victimisation can include things like being ignored, given unfair treatment, or being bullied because of your involvement in a discrimination issue.

Employers have a responsibility to prevent victimisation and to handle any complaints seriously and fairly. If you believe you are being victimised at work, you can raise the issue informally with your employer or follow a formal complaint procedure.

Step 1: Keep a record of any incidents of victimisation.
Step 2: Report the victimisation to your employer or HR department.
Step 3: If the issue is not resolved, consider seeking advice from ACAS or Citizens Advice.

Sources used:
- https://www.acas.org.uk/discrimination-bullying-and-harassment
- https://www.gov.uk/discrimination-your-rights

question: My employer ignored my complaint about discrimination. What can I do?
answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

If your employer ignores your complaint about discrimination, you have several options. First, you can raise the issue informally by discussing it with your manager or HR department. If this does not resolve the problem, you can make a formal complaint through your employer’s grievance procedure. Employers are legally required to take discrimination complaints seriously and investigate them properly.

If your employer still fails to address your complaint, you may consider seeking advice from an external organisation such as ACAS or Citizens Advice. You can also make a claim to an employment tribunal for discrimination under the Equality Act 2010. It is important to keep records of your complaint and any responses from your employer.

Step 1: Raise the issue informally with your employer.  
Step 2: Submit a formal grievance if informal steps fail.  
Step 3: Seek advice from ACAS or Citizens Advice.  
Step 4: Consider making a claim to an employment tribunal if necessary.

Sources used:
- https://www.acas.org.uk/discrimination-bullying-and-harassment
- https://www.gov.uk/discrimination-your-rights

question: Is bullying at work illegal?
answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

Bullying at work is not a specific criminal offence, but it can be unlawful if it amounts to discrimination, harassment, or victimisation under the Equality Act 2010. Employers have a legal duty to prevent bullying and to take complaints seriously. If bullying is related to a protected characteristic such as age, race, sex, disability, or religion, it may be considered unlawful discrimination.

If you experience or witness bullying, you can raise the issue informally or formally with your employer. Employers should have policies and procedures in place to handle bullying and discrimination complaints and are expected to investigate and address such issues appropriately.

Step 1: Keep a record of incidents of bullying, including dates, times, and what happened.  
Step 2: Report the bullying to your employer, either informally or through a formal complaint.  
Step 3: If the employer does not resolve the issue, you may seek advice from ACAS or Citizens Advice about your options.

Sources used:
- https://www.acas.org.uk/discrimination-bullying-and-harassment

question: What counts as harassment at work?
answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

Harassment at work is unwanted behaviour linked to a protected characteristic that violates a person's dignity or creates an intimidating, hostile, degrading, humiliating, or offensive environment. This can include things like offensive jokes, name-calling, physical attacks, or unwanted physical contact. Harassment can be direct or indirect and is covered under the Equality Act 2010, which protects characteristics such as age, sex, race, disability, religion, and others.

Employers have a responsibility to prevent harassment and to take complaints seriously if they arise. If you experience harassment, you can raise the issue informally or formally with your employer, who should have procedures in place to handle such complaints.

Step 1: Recognise if the behaviour is unwanted and linked to a protected characteristic.  
Step 2: Report the harassment to your employer, either informally or through a formal complaint.  
Step 3: If the employer does not address the issue, you may seek advice from ACAS or Citizens Advice.

Sources used:
- https://www.acas.org.uk/discrimination-bullying-and-harassment
- https://www.gov.uk/discrimination-your-rights

question: Can my employer refuse reasonable adjustments for my disability?
answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

Under UK law, specifically the Equality Act 2010, employers have a legal duty to make reasonable adjustments for employees with disabilities to help them overcome disadvantages at work. This means your employer should consider and implement changes that enable you to do your job effectively, such as modifying equipment, altering work hours, or adjusting duties. However, the adjustments must be "reasonable," which depends on factors like the cost, practicality, and impact on the business.

If your employer refuses to make reasonable adjustments without a valid reason, this could be considered discrimination under the Equality Act 2010. You can raise the issue informally with your employer or follow formal grievance procedures. If unresolved, you may have grounds to bring a claim to an employment tribunal.

Step 1: Inform your employer about your disability and the adjustments you need.  
Step 2: Discuss possible reasonable adjustments and how they can be implemented.  
Step 3: If refused, ask for a clear explanation and consider raising a formal grievance.  
Step 4: Seek advice from ACAS or Citizens Advice if the issue remains unresolved.

Sources used:
- https://www.gov.uk/discrimination-your-rights
- https://www.acas.org.uk/discrimination-bullying-and-harassment

question: I was treated unfairly because of my race. What are my rights?
answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

Under UK law, specifically the Equality Act 2010, it is illegal for an employer to discriminate against you because of your race, which includes your colour, nationality, or ethnic or national origin. You are protected from discrimination at work, meaning your employer must not treat you unfairly or less favourably because of your race. This protection also covers situations where you have complained about discrimination or supported someone else’s claim.

If you believe you have been treated unfairly due to your race, you can raise the issue with your employer, either informally or through a formal complaint procedure. Employers are required to take such complaints seriously and take steps to prevent discrimination. If the issue is not resolved internally, you may have the option to bring a claim to an employment tribunal.

Step 1: Document the incidents where you believe you were treated unfairly because of your race.  
Step 2: Raise the issue with your employer, following their grievance procedure if they have one.  
Step 3: If the problem is not resolved, consider seeking advice from ACAS or Citizens Advice about making a formal claim.

Sources used:
- https://www.gov.uk/discrimination-your-rights
- https://www.acas.org.uk/discrimination-bullying-and-harassment

question: Can I be dismissed for making a discrimination complaint?
answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

In the UK, it is generally unlawful for an employer to dismiss you because you have made a complaint about discrimination. Such a dismissal could be considered automatically unfair and may also amount to victimisation under the Equality Act 2010. Employers must not treat employees less favourably for asserting their rights or making a discrimination complaint.

If you believe you have been dismissed for making a discrimination complaint, you may have grounds to bring a claim for unfair dismissal or discrimination to an employment tribunal. It is important that your employer follows a fair and reasonable procedure in any dismissal, including giving valid reasons and allowing you to appeal.

Step 1: Keep records of your complaint and any related communications.  
Step 2: Raise your concerns formally with your employer if you have not already done so.  
Step 3: Seek advice from ACAS, Citizens Advice, or a qualified solicitor to understand your options and the time limits for making a claim.

Sources used:
- https://www.gov.uk/dismissal
- https://www.acas.org.uk/dismissals

question: What should I do if I experience harassment at work?
answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

If you experience harassment at work, your employer has a legal duty to prevent it and take any complaints seriously under the Equality Act 2010. Harassment can include unwanted behaviour related to protected characteristics such as age, sex, race, disability, or religion. You can choose to address the issue informally by speaking to your manager or HR, or formally by making a written complaint following your employer’s grievance procedure.

It is important to keep a record of incidents, including dates, times, what happened, and any witnesses. If your employer does not resolve the issue, you may be able to take your complaint to an employment tribunal. Employers should have policies in place to handle harassment and bullying complaints and must act to stop it happening.

Step 1: Keep a detailed record of the harassment incidents.  
Step 2: Report the harassment to your employer, either informally or formally.  
Step 3: Follow your employer’s grievance procedure if informal steps do not work.  
Step 4: Seek advice from ACAS or Citizens Advice if you need further help or if the issue is not resolved.

Sources used:
- https://www.acas.org.uk/discrimination-bullying-and-harassment
- https://www.gov.uk/discrimination-your-rights
